name: Ashoka
usesInAyurveda: Tridoshic Modulation: Prominently pacifies Pitta and Kapha. Its extreme Kashaya (astringent) Rasa and Sheeta (cooling) Virya act as a biological sponge, absorbing excess Pitta-driven bleeding and Kapha-driven cysts.
Karma (Actions): Garbhashaya Doshahara (Uterine tonic), Raktastambhaka (Hemostatic), Shothaghna (Anti-inflammatory), Vedanasthapana (Analgesic for uterine cramps).
API/AFI Adherence: The dried stem bark is the API official part. It forms the foundation of Ashokarishta and Ashoka Ghrita.
Specific Indications: Asrigdara (Menorrhagia/heavy bleeding), Shwetapradara (Leucorrhea), Kashtartava (Dysmenorrhea/painful periods), Garbhashaya Arbuda (Uterine Fibroids).
usageForm: Stem bark decoction (Kwatha), Fermented preparation (Ashokarishta), Medicated Ghee.
mainMedicinalUses: Supreme Uterine Tonic (Garbhashaya Doshahara): 'Ashoka' literally translates to 'remover of sorrow' for women. It stimulates the endometrium and ovarian tissues, regulating endogenous hormonal cascades to treat PCOD, heavy menstrual bleeding, and severe pelvic pain.
Anti-Hemorrhagic (Raktastambhaka): The extreme astringency rapidly constricts severely bleeding capillary vessels in the uterus, treating Menorrhagia and dysbiosis-driven Leucorrhea.
Fibroid Management: Used heavily in clinical Ayurveda to shrink uterine and ovarian cysts (Arbuda/Granthi) by physically scraping away stagnant Kapha and blocking inflammatory Pitta markers.
plantProfile: Unique History: Worshiped as a profoundly sacred tree across Hinduism and Buddhism (where Queen Maya gave birth to Gautama Buddha under an Ashoka tree). It is the unquestioned pillar of Ayurvedic gynecology.

Physical Characteristics: A majestic, small evergreen tree with beautiful, dense clusters of highly fragrant, orange-yellow turning to crimson flowers. Sadly, it is categorized as highly vulnerable/endangered in the wild.
Specific Chemical Compounds: Rich in tannins, catechins, flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol), and sterols. Tannins represent its extreme astringent properties.
Strict Standardizations: The most heavily adulterated plant in India (often replaced by Polyalthia longifolia). API mandates strict microscopic bark identification and tannin quantification.
preparationMethods: Ksheerapaka (Milk Decoction): Boiling Ashoka bark powder in milk and water until only milk remains. This is the absolute peak clinical method for treating heavy menstrual bleeding (Asrigdara).
Ashokarishta: The classical fermented wine containing Ashoka, prescribed universally by Ayurvedic physicians for female reproductive stabilization.

dosages.Children: Generally not applicable unless specifically directed for early-onset gynecological imbalances.
dosages.Adults: Ksheerapaka Base: 5-10g bark powder. Ashokarishta: 15-30ml mixed with an equal volume of water post-meals.
dosages.Elderly: Effective for managing post-menopausal bleeding or pelvic prolapse inflammation.

safetyProfile: Contraindications: Amenorrhea (absence of periods)—due to its severely astringent, flow-stopping nature, it will worsen absentee periods.
GRAS Status: Recognized globally with a spotless, millennial-long safety record.
Adverse Effects: Extreme overdosing may cause severe constipation due to its intense tannins.
